questions.q: What does `vec.get(i)` return that `vec[i]` does not, and why is this the safer choice for potentially-out-of-bounds access?
questions.a: `.get(i)` returns `Option<&T>` — `None` if the index is out of bounds. Direct indexing with `vec[i]` panics at runtime if `i` is out of range. `.get()` forces the caller to handle the missing case explicitly.

questions.q: Explain the HashMap entry API: what does `.entry(k).or_insert(v)` do when the key is present vs absent?
questions.a: When the key is absent, it inserts the provided value `v` and returns a `&mut` to it. When the key is present, it skips the insertion and returns a `&mut` to the existing value. Either way the return is a mutable reference, which lets you increment in place.

questions.q: Why does Rust disallow `my_string[0]` for a String, unlike arrays or Vec?
questions.a: String is UTF-8 encoded, and characters can span 1–4 bytes. Indexing by integer could land in the middle of a multi-byte character, producing invalid data. Rust refuses this at compile time; use `.chars().nth(i)` for characters or `.bytes().nth(i)` for raw bytes.

questions.q: What is the difference between `.iter()` and `.into_iter()` on a Vec?
questions.a: `.iter()` borrows the Vec and yields `&T` references — the Vec remains usable after. `.into_iter()` consumes the Vec and yields owned `T` values — the Vec is no longer accessible after the call.
